summary: | | Wuerch defends diversity STATE OF THE CITY: Mayor addresses racism fight, gay exhibit removal. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published June 26, 2001) Mayor George Wuerch described himself as a defender of diversity and an opponent of racism Monday while at the same time defending his decision to not allow a gay rights display at the city's main library. Wuerch's approach to multiculturalism figured prominently in his 40-minute State of the City speech at the 4th Avenue Theatre. title: Charter hits rock, halts trip
summary: Juneau -- A well-known nature photographer and members of his photography workshop cruising along a Southeast waterway early Thursday in search of whales and wildlife spent the day on a Coast Guard rescue ship after a rock punched a hole in their charter vessel. Art Wolfe of Seattle, author of 40 photography books, and seven other passengers and three crew members were safely taken aboard the U.S. Coast Guard cutter Liberty after the captain of their 96-foot charter boat radioed a mayday call about 8 a.m., said Petty Officer Darrell Wilson. No one was injured, he said. The vessel, the Alaskan Song, was refloated by 3:15 p.m. and towed to Ketchikan for repairs, Wilson said. title: Cruise ship bill stalls
summary: Juneau -- A bill that would dramatically beef up environmental regulation of Alaska's booming cruise ship industry hit a wall Sunday when a committee chairman announced he doesn't plan to allow the measure out of his panel. "I doubt we'll get through it this year," said Sen. John Cowdery, chairman of the Senate Transportation Committee, where HB 260 has stalled. "If it leaves my committee, it will be a good bill," said Cowdery, R-Anchorage. "It's not a good bill now, and I don't believe in passing it out and let the next committee straighten it out." Among the problems, Cowdery said, is that the measure unfairly holds smaller vessels to the same environmental standards as huge cruise ships. title: Alaska oil price takes a dive
summary: Shopping for a cheap barrel of oil? Look no farther than Alaska's finest. North Slope crude oil sold for as low as $20.09 a barrel this week, making it the cheapest crude in the world. Alaska oil is selling at a deep discount compared with other crudes because of a confluence of peculiar factors in the West Coast markets, including increased Middle East oil flow, a seasonal slump in gasoline demand and low water flow in Western rivers. Those factors have expanded supply and caused a contraction in demand on the West Coast. "It's weird," said Sam Van Vactor, owner of energy consulting firm Economic Insight Inc. in Porland, Ore. "I've never seen anything quite like it." In the past six weeks, crude oil prices around the world have fallen roughly 25 percent primarily because of increased OPEC…

title: Stranded dipnetters evacuated
summary: Wood Canyon -- The heroes drove heavy equipment on Monday. Despite the rotten cliffs of the Copper River, raining down rock and mud and ice, equipment operators punched open the rugged Copper River Road on Monday afternoon to free 200 to 300 salmon dipnetters trapped by a landslide a day and a half earlier. But even as grateful fishermen with their coolers of salmon rolled out from the canyon, the slide dumped debris on the newly plowed route. Alaska State Troopers and the state Department of Transportation ordered everyone to evacuate north of O'Brien Creek. Transportation district manager George Lavasseuer said the state planned to temporarily close the road to try to determine how to stabilize the collapsing slope, near an old U.S Geological Survey gauging station about 3.5 miles south …

summary: People living in rural Alaska communities are increasingly becoming obese and diabetic or they've got a mouthful of rotten teeth. "You have 32 teeth maximum in your mouth, 28 until you get your wisdom teeth," said Dr. Rowena Mandanas, a Nome dentist with the U.S. Public Health Service. "I've been to villages where kids have 19 cavities." Mandanas works with schools in the Nome area to distribute toothbrushes and toothpaste to students and to make sure the students are using them. There's a lot of tooth decay in her area because many people don't brush their teeth, she said. Drinking soda means even more problems. title: Chicken recipe makes a name for itself and for Alaska cook
summary: Marti Bradley has a distinct recollection of her first adventure in cooking. She was about 7 and wanted to make herself of cup of cocoa. This was in the old days, before genetic engineering and instant hot chocolate. She did fine measuring and mixing the cocoa powder, sugar and milk. But then the directions called for a dash of salt. I didn't know what a dash was so I thought they meant a dish," Bradley said. "It was pretty gruesome." She never cooked again until she had to, and that wasn't until she was married with kids. Later, when she was unmarried with kids, it became critical to make cooking an event to celebrate rather than dread. As it turns out, the Steller sea lion is saving the Alaska SeaLife Center. summary: Kodiak -- Federal officials have approved 26 research projects to examine the decline of the Steller sea lion in the oceans off western Alaska. The projects were approved by Jim Balsiger, National Marine Fisheries Service administrator for the Alaska region, but the list is still subject to review by grant administrators with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. summary: Kodiak -- Farmers have a long history of being able to insure their crops against losses caused by drought, insects or other calamities, but commercial fishermen who face poor harvests have never been included in any insurance plans. That could soon change. Last year, Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, got salmon officially designated as a harvested food crop. That meant a salmon provision was tagged onto crop insurance, and a $1 million federal subsidy was designated for fishermen to insure their catches. Now, to figure out how crop insurance might be applied to fisheries, scoping meetings are being launched this month by University of Alaska Fairbanks researchers and U.S. Department of Agriculture risk management agents. summary: Here's the way it usually works: a government, handed the bounty from natural resources within its borders, spends the money as fast as it flows in. Alaska, through its oil-wealth Permanent Fund savings account, is among a small cadre of places that tried to sock away some of today's wealth for another day. A Norway-based economist recently studied the savings attempts by Alaska and three other places: Alberta, Norway and a tiny Pacific island nation called Nauru that was rich in phosphate. The economist, Rognvaldur Hannesson, explored a decades-old model devised by Nobel laureate Sir John Hicks that shows how temporary mineral wealth -- particularly from the lucrative oil business -- can be invested in a way that will make it last forever while still providing money for government service…

By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published June 24, 2001) Millions of prime red salmon are about to surge into remote Bristol Bay, but commercial fisherman George Pearson won't be there to chase them. title: Kodiak fish plant hauls in big fine
summary: A Kodiak fish-processing company pleaded guilty Friday to a federal felony charge of illegally underreporting the amount of pollock purchased from a fishing boat. International Seafoods of Alaska Inc., whose plant is one of the biggest in the commercial fishing hub of Kodiak, was fined $150,000 and put on probation for five years under a plea agreement with prosecutors. The company also must pay another $46,000, the estimated market value of pollock illegally received at the plant in the first three months of 1999. And it must run ads in the Maine-based trade journal National Fisherman acknowledging its guilt and the importance of the fishing industry making accurate catch and production reports to government fishery managers looking after the health of ocean fish stocks. U.S. District Judge Edward J. Lodge ruled Thursday that a Clinton administration rule banning new roads and logging on roadless portions of national forests needed to be amended or it would cause irreparable harm. summary: Environmentalists have asked a federal judge in Anchorage to reinstate a logging ban in roadless areas of Southeast's Tongass National Forest. Earthjustice Legal Defense Fund filed the motion Friday on behalf of the Sitka Conservation Society, Sierra Club, Alaska Center for the Environment, and The Wilderness Society. Singleton agreed that the Forest Service, in updating the Tongass management plan, violated the National Environmental Policy Act and the National Forest Management Act by failing to evaluate 9.4 million roadless acres for possible wilderness designation by Congress.
